questions.question_text: In South Dakota, residential closings are typically handled by:
questions.explanation: South Dakota closings are typically conducted by title companies or attorneys. They can take place at the broker's office, title company, or lending agency, and may be conducted as round-table (all parties present) or by mail/escrow.

questions.question_text: A closing where all parties are present at the same location is called a:
questions.explanation: South Dakota allows both round table closings (all parties present) and closings by mail or escrow. Round table closings allow immediate document signing, question answering, and fund transfers in a single meeting.

questions.question_text: In South Dakota, closings may be conducted at which of the following locations?
questions.correct_answer: Broker's office, title company, or lender
distractors: Only at the county Register of Deeds
Only at the buyer's attorney's office
questions.explanation: South Dakota closings can take place at the broker's office, the title company, or the lending institution. The state allows flexibility in closing locations, and closings may be conducted in person (round table) or by mail/escrow.

questions.question_text: South Dakota property taxes are assessed one year and due the next. This system is called:
distractors: Taxes paid at point of sale
questions.explanation: South Dakota property taxes are paid in arrears — they are assessed in one calendar year and due the following year in two installments (April 30 and October 31). This affects how taxes are prorated between buyer and seller at closing.

questions.question_text: A title search reveals a mechanic's lien on the property. This would be classified as a:
questions.correct_answer: Title defect that must be resolved before closing
distractors: Normal encumbrance that the buyer must accept
Matter only for the lender to address
questions.explanation: A mechanic's lien is a title defect — an encumbrance that would appear on the title search. Title companies examine recorded documents for liens, encumbrances, and defects to ensure clear title can be conveyed to the buyer at closing.

questions.question_text: What is the South Dakota real estate transfer fee rate?
questions.explanation: South Dakota charges a transfer fee of $0.50 per $500 of value (or fraction thereof), which works out to approximately 0.1%. The fee is paid by the grantor (seller) and collected by the Register of Deeds at recording.

questions.question_text: Who is responsible for paying the real estate transfer fee in South Dakota?
questions.explanation: The transfer fee is paid by the grantor (seller) and collected by the Register of Deeds at the time of recording. The amount is noted on the face of the deed after recording.

questions.question_text: South Dakota property taxes are paid in arrears. When are tax installments due?
questions.explanation: SD property taxes are paid in arrears (assessed one year, due the next) with installments due April 30 and October 31. This affects how taxes are prorated at closing — the seller typically owes for the portion of the year they occupied the property.

questions.question_text: South Dakota follows which type of recording system?
questions.explanation: South Dakota uses a race-notice recording system. Under this system, a subsequent good-faith purchaser who records first prevails over a prior unrecorded interest. Documents are filed with the county Register of Deeds.

questions.question_text: A government agency transfers property to another government entity. Is the transfer fee required?
questions.correct_answer: No, government transfers are exempt from the fee
distractors: Yes, all transfers require the full fee amount
Yes, but at a reduced rate of 50% of normal
No, but only federal government agencies qualify
questions.explanation: Transfers by or to government entities are exempt from the SD transfer fee under SDCL 43-4-22. Other exemptions include corporate dissolution transfers, estate distributions, and related entity transfers. Exempt documents must cite the applicable subsection.
